ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Hosting regional and international study visits; I3 deliberation processes: Participatory democracy forums; I5 investment activities: Infrastructure development (solar, organic market gardens, community farm, eco-construction); I7 self organizing activities: Cooperative/local agricultural support and eco-building reconstruction; I8 networking activities: Connecting with international Transition groups and film-makers
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Transition to participatory democracy and Fair Trade designation; O2 ecological performance measures: Reduction of transport emissions via horse carriage; generation of significant local solar energy; O3 externalities to other SESs: Inspired other communities across France and internationally

p3_items.verbatim_evidence: began there in 2014 after then-Mayor Jean-Claude Mensch showed his team the film ‘In Transition 2.0’ and, as the legend goes, said 'let’s do that, all of that'.

post_title: Guest post: Summary of the Inaugural Planning Meeting of the Borough of What If
practice_name: Inaugural Planning Meeting of the Borough of What If

ostrom_summary.governance: GS5 operational rules: Using 'Imagine it, long for it, live it' as a general rule to live by.
ostrom_summary.users: U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Dr Lucy Wallace and Mr Rob Hopkins; U9 technology used: Quantum technology for presentations, bubbles for space-time continuum stabilization
ostrom_summary.interactions: I3 deliberation processes: Splitting into exploratory departments ('Wellbeing', 'Education', 'Social Programmes'/'Grow and Flow') and then disbanding them to prevent silos.; I7 self organizing activities: Building a metaphorical time machine using on-hand materials and collective imagination to open a portal to 2036.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Realization of deep interconnection between planning departments and formal request for more collective imagining opportunities.

p1_justification: The practice utilizes open-ended 'what if' scenarios and collective social dreaming to explore potential futures, rather than relying on predictive forecasting or rigid blueprints.
p1_verbatim_evidence: The Planning Committee decided to concentrate their efforts on splitting up and exploring the most important aspects of the Borough of What If... The Departments of ‘Wellbeing’, ‘Education’ and ‘Social Programmes’ were duly formed
p2_justification: The methodology relies on narrative storytelling and speculative design artifacts—specifically constructing a metaphorical time machine from local materials—to travel to the year 2036.
p2_verbatim_evidence: The Planning Committee used materials they had on-hand and a healthy dose of imagination to build the required time machine and open the portal to 2036.

p3_items.justification: The practice is oriented toward activating participants' capacities for social dreaming and collective imagining to explore new civic possibilities.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The Planning Committee want to put in a formal request for more time travel for more people, as we feel that it is important for many others to have the opportunity to experience collective imagining

p3_items.justification: The workshop fosters an active, forward-looking orientation that translates imagined futures into an active rule to live by in the present.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: the general consensus was that it might help to start using “Imagine it, long for it, live it” as a general rule to live by.

post_title: Ministry of Imagination Manifesto released as the world goes to the polls.
practice_name: From What If to What Next podcast & The Ministry of Imagination Manifesto

ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Supported by multiple organizations (Boomtown Festival, Wake the Tiger, Team Love, Bath Spa University, Moral Imaginations, and Transition Network)
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: An eclectic mix including ecologists, renegade economists, artists, prison abolitionists, somatic trauma therapists, printmakers, politicians, disability activists, and rewilding practitioners; U3 history of use: Podcast and community active from 2020 to March 2024; U4 location: Global; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Rob Hopkins (podcast host and editor), Tamzin Pinkerton (editing helper), and Capella Andrean (designer)
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Patreon subscribers sharing transformative policy ideas via bonus podcasts; I3 deliberation processes: Active policy suggestion and selection during the podcast; I5 investment activities: Production costs covered by sponsorship; seeking public donations for printing copies; I8 networking activities: Partnering with festivals, universities, and activist networks
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Over 600 ambitious, radical transition policies collected and published as a collaborative manifesto

p1_justification: The practice is built around open-ended 'what if' scenarios to generate collaborative ideas for alternative futures, explicitly rejecting forecasting in favor of social dreaming and public discussion.
p1_verbatim_evidence: Since 2020, the fortnightly ‘From What If to What Next’ podcast... 'The Ministry of Imagination Manifesto: an imagination-based manifesto for times that need one' was born.

p3_items.justification: The project is grounded in imaginative provocation, utilizing speculative 'what if' questions to unlock the collective imagination of its audience and generate policies for alternative futures.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: invited to choose 3 policies each that would rapidly accelerate our transition to a world in which those changes had happened.

p3_items.justification: The practice leverages radical imagination to counteract apocalyptic and status-quo thinking, compiling hundreds of hopeful and audacious policies that promote equitable, liberatory futures.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The result was over 600 deeply thoughtful, considered, audacious and ambitious policies covering everything from free art materials for all, to a Universal Basic Income... And so 'The Ministry of Imagination Manifesto: an imagination-based manifesto for times that need one' was born.

p3_items.justification: The podcast explicitly transforms passive listeners into active participants and 'Ministers' who are given the agency to choose and shape policy directions for societal transition.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Patreon subscribers also received bonus Ministry of Imagination podcasts, in which they were inaugurated as Ministers at the Ministry itself (part-Hundertwasser, part-Hogwarts, part-Yellow Submarine) and invited to choose 3 policies each...

post_title: United by the flow of the River Exe

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: River / Freshwater system; RS3 size of resource system: The River Exe and its tributaries; RS4 human constructed facilities: Exmouth sewage plant and privatised water infrastructure; RS9 location: River Exe, Devon, England
ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Transition groups (Sustainable Tiverton, Transition Exeter, and Transition Exmouth); GS3 network structure: Partnership model connecting local Transition groups, landowners, other conservation trusts, and community members; GS5 operational rules: Vision statement serving as an open membership rule; GS8 monitoring and sanctioning processes: Volunteer-led water quality monitoring / citizen science
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: Over 200 active community participants; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Diverse groups including swimmers, surfers, environmentalists, canoeists, farmers, litter pickers, climate activists, landowners, and anglers; U4 location: Along the River Exe and its tributaries; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Nicky Nicholls (Transition Exmouth) and hired project staff; U6 norms social capital: Shared stewardship, mutual support pledges, and bridging conflicts between historically opposing user groups
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: River Conversations community events; I3 deliberation processes: Collaborative meetings to establish common values and working groups; I4 conflicts among users: Mediation and integration of groups with differing uses of the river (e.g., anglers, landowners, swimmers); I5 investment activities: Securing Transition Together seed funding from the National Lottery Community Fund; I7 self organizing activities: Forming the 'Friends of the River Exe' steering group and core working groups; I8 networking activities: Linking multiple local Transition groups and regional environmental trusts
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Establishment of diverse action groups, volunteer growth, and creation of a River Charter

p1_justification: The practice represents a material, collaborative effort to establish a holistic, affirmative alternative to the degradation of the River Exe, focusing on active stewardship, protection, and a shared vision of community care.
p1_verbatim_evidence: A Zoom call was convened between people from Sustainable Tiverton, Transition Exeter and Transition Exmouth to explore the possibility of coming together to create a collaborative effort to improve the river. And so Friends of the River Exe was born.

p3_items.justification: The initiative cultivates an active, learned capacity for hope and stewardship, encouraging community members to translate their concerns into forward-looking pledges to care for the river's future.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Over 200 people came out to participate in ‘River Conversations’ and share ideas and pledges to protect, restore and celebrate the River Exe.

p3_items.justification: The practice structures active social change and conscientization through self-organized working groups that engage in political, legal, and ecological education to protect the river.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: From this, different working groups formed, looking at different areas like legal rights, direct action, citizen science/water quality monitoring or hands-on conservation projects

post_title: Travels in my Time Machine Part Three: visiting the Cornish beavers of 2030
location_string: Chris Jones's farm near Laddock, Cornwall

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Wetland and freshwater ecosystem restoration; RS3 size of resource system: 200 metres for one pair, extending across the whole catchment; RS4 human constructed facilities: Enclosures; RS5 productivity of system: Abundant water retention during extremes, massive wildlife hosting, and heavy silt trapping; RS8 storage characteristics: A series of beaver-created ponds storing water and trapping silt; RS9 location: Near Laddock, Cornwall, UK
ostrom_summary.governance: GS4 property rights systems: Private farm ownership
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Farmers and tourists/visitors; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Chris Jones; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Knowledge of the beavers' hydrological capabilities, ecological benefits, and capacity to prevent localized flooding
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Farm tours to educate the public; I4 conflicts among users: Low levels of conflict with local farming; I5 investment activities: Reintroducing and releasing beavers into enclosures and catchments; I8 networking activities: Inspiring similar beaver reintroduction projects across other catchments in the country
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Recreational value from tourism and community re-enchantment; O2 ecological performance measures: Explosion of biodiversity (including fish size octupling), water retention, natural flood management, and heavy silt trapping; O3 externalities to other SESs: Widespread replication and inspiration for beaver reintroduction in other catchments

p1_justification: The practice represents a material, affirmative ecological alternative that actively restores biodiversity and hydrological functions, aiming to overcome environmental degradation and achieve a reenchanted relationship with the landscape.
p1_verbatim_evidence: The moment the first saw an actual beaver moving through the water was just magical, a reenchantment of the landscape.

p3_items.justification: The practice utilizes open-ended 'what if' questions to prompt speculative visualization of ecological restoration across an entire catchment area.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: What if we had 10 pairs of beavers across the whole catchment? I don't think it (Laddock...) would ever flood.

p3_items.justification: The practice fosters a sense of active, anticipatory hope and community re-enchantment by proving that ecological transformation is possible and inspiring others to take action.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: to see that the idea has now transformed his own catchment also inspired the same in many others across the country.

post_title: When Imagination Activists and graphic artists join forces
location_string: River Culm and its catchment, Devon countryside

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Water management and river catchment conservation; RS4 human constructed facilities: Cycle paths on old railway tracks, pathways, and maintained river banks; RS9 location: River Culm catchment, Devon, United Kingdom
ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: Blackdown Hills Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty; GS2 nongovernment organizations: Connecting the Culm (CtC); GS3 network structure: Wider international partnership under the Co-Adapt project
ostrom_summary.users: U4 location: Communities living along the River Culm catchment; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Dominic Acland, Tim Youngs, Rob Hopkins, and Richard Carman; U6 norms social capital: Community collaboration, taking on responsibility for natural resources, and consensus building; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Using 'Time Travel' workshops to imagine sustainable and resilient ecological states; U9 technology used: Zoom for interactive virtual workshops and rapid graphic visualization
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Imaginative visioning, group sharing, and graphical feedback loops; I3 deliberation processes: Community consultation workshops and collaborative blueprint development; I7 self organizing activities: Communities organizing to take responsibility for river bank maintenance; I8 networking activities: International networking through the EU-funded Co-Adapt project
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: The launching of a community-endorsed Blueprint plan for the river; O2 ecological performance measures: Future resilience to floods and drought, and improved local biodiversity and water quality

p1_justification: The practice employs open-ended time-travel dreaming to conceptualize future possibilities for the River Culm catchment, bypassing predictive forecasting in favor of participatory, imaginative visioning to build a community blueprint.
p1_verbatim_evidence: we ran a series of workshops over Zoom where the people participating travelled to 2050 in my Time Machine, to a River Culm catchment that is now sustainable, resilient to flooding and drought
p2_justification: The practice uses narrative time-travel exercises combined with live, rapid graphic design and sketching to co-create and debate alternative future states of the catchment system.
p2_verbatim_evidence: After the groups had time travelled in their imaginations, I put them into pairs to reflect on what they saw, smelt, felt, heard and tasted, and then to feed that back. While this happened, Richard started drawing. He drew fast, impressionistic sketches to try and capture what people were saying.

p3_items.justification: The practice fosters an active, anticipatory consciousness by encouraging participants to believe in and plan for a sustainable, resilient future, translating this forward-dreaming hope into a concrete, community-endorsed action plan.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: create a long-term plan for the future of the River Culm that everyone is signed up to – the Blueprint for the River Culm

p3_items.justification: The workshops leverage sensory imagination and rapid visual feedback to provoke active social dreaming, allowing participants to explore and refine alternative visions of their local environment.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: After the groups had time travelled in their imaginations, I put them into pairs to reflect on what they saw, smelt, felt, heard and tasted, and then to feed that back. While this happened, Richard started drawing... so he could ask people 'you mean like this? Is there anything needs changing or adding?'

post_title: Travels in my Time Machine Part One: The Vauban.

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Residential neighborhood and ecological district; RS2 clarity of system boundaries: 41 hectares of land; RS3 size of resource system: 41 hectares; RS4 human constructed facilities: Passivhaus buildings, solar panels, tram system, central community car park, swale, Combined Heat and Power scheme, community centre, school, nurseries; RS9 location: Freiburg, Germany
ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Housing cooperatives, individual self builders, and community building groups; GS4 property rights systems: Prioritization of cooperatives and community builders over commercial developers; GS5 operational rules: Drop-off only car access at home doors; permanent car parking restricted to the central community car park; GS6 collective choice rules: Inclusive design processes that incorporated future residents' ideas, social concerns, and ecological dreams
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: About 3,000 residents; U4 location: The Vauban district, Freiburg, Germany; U6 norms social capital: Strong sense of community, campaigning, and community building; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: High ecological design awareness and prioritization of social and ecological concerns; U9 technology used: Tram system, cargo bikes, Passivhaus design, solar power, Combined Heat and Power scheme
ostrom_summary.interactions: I3 deliberation processes: Inclusive resident design processes addressing future dreams and ecological/social concerns; I5 investment activities: Investment by cooperatives and individual self-builders in high-efficiency housing; I6 lobbying activities: Three-year campaign for a community-led development model on the former barracks; I7 self organizing activities: Citizen campaigns, collaborative design, self-building, and cooperative management
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Low private car ownership, high bike/tram usage, safe spaces for children's play, strong community cohesion; O2 ecological performance measures: Passivhaus-level energy efficiency, high solar energy generation, stormwater return via swale, high urban canopy and biodiversity preservation

p1_justification: The Vauban represents a realized, affirmative alternative to conventional carbon-intensive and developer-led urban spaces, focusing on tangible social and ecological transformations.
p1_verbatim_evidence: The final scheme prioritised housing cooperatives, individual self builders and community building groups over private commercial developers and investors. ... The final scheme shows what’s possible when ecological design is put front and centre.

p3_items.justification: The design process engaged the community in social dreaming by centering the dreams and ideas of future residents to shape their environment.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The design process was inclusive of future residents’ ideas and dreams, and was expanded to include social and ecological issues of concern to residents.

p3_items.justification: The project serves as a real-world demonstration of sustainable living, nurturing educated hope by showing that alternative ecological futures are practically achievable.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The final scheme shows what’s possible when ecological design is put front and centre.

post_title: A delicious immersion in the fantastic dream world of Luc Schuiten.

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Eco-architecture and regenerative urban design; RS4 human constructed facilities: An architectural studio located inside a tall, elegant city house, which features customized creative elements like a wooden bath and a mechanical treetop meditation seat in the garden.; RS9 location: Brussels, Belgium
ostrom_summary.users: U4 location: Brussels, Belgium; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Luc Schuiten; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: A regenerative future shaped by biomimicry and 'vegetal cities' rooted in care, equality, and natural patterns.; U9 technology used: Mushroom-based materials, biomimicry designs, hybrid pedal/electric transport, and neural-responsive meditation pulleys.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Creating and sharing visioning drawings of vegetal cities, hosting visitors, and doing interviews to disseminate ideas.; I3 deliberation processes: Intimate discussions and idea formulation in a custom-designed double wooden bath.

p1_justification: The practice presents a material, holistic, and affirmative alternative to modern cities, imagining future urban spaces as living, regenerative systems rooted in biomimicry and kindness.
p1_verbatim_evidence: They try to represent what our towns and cities could look like in 2100 if they were the result of a process of being transformed into what he calls ‘vegetal cities’, living, regenerative places shaped by biomimicry, the shapes and patterns of nature, and by a society more rooted in kindness, care and equality than the one we live in today.

p3_items.justification: The practice cultivates educated hope by providing tangible, forward-looking representations of how a highly ecological and regenerative future can be achieved.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Luc is an architect who designs extraordinary ecological buildings and who also has become very well known, mostly in the French-speaking world, for his incredible images showing what an ecological, regenerative future could look like in about 100 years.

p3_items.justification: Schuiten's practice uses highly detailed visual design as a tool for social dreaming, sparking deep discussions about potential future trajectories for human societies.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: He talks us through his drawings and we are straight into a deep and rich conversation about his work... They try to represent what our towns and cities could look like in 2100 if they were the result of a process of being transformed into what he calls ‘vegetal cities’

post_title: Special podcast: ‘A 2030 to long for’. And a theory to share.

ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: Many different, diverse groups of people; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Rob Hopkins
ostrom_summary.interactions: I3 deliberation processes: Inviting participants to safe spaces to imagine traveling to 2030, step into that world, and come back to describe it.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Reveals common humanity and a shared longing for contentment and happiness

p1_justification: The practice utilizes a 'Time Machine' metaphor to invite participants into an open-ended scenario where they can step into and describe the year 2030, exploring multiple potential versions of the future rather than a fixed prediction.
p1_verbatim_evidence: I tell people that I have a Time Machine, and that I am inviting them to travel with me to 2030. ... And I invite them to step into that world, to take a walk and to come back and describe it.
p2_justification: The practice is a speculative storytelling exercise that asks participants to imaginatively travel to 2030, step into that world, and describe it as a way to explore alternative futures.
p2_verbatim_evidence: I tell people that I have a Time Machine, and that I am inviting them to travel with me to 2030. ... And I invite them to step into that world, to take a walk and to come back and describe it.

p3_items.justification: The exercise serves as an imaginative provocation that unlocks participants' capacity for social dreaming, guiding them to visualize what a future world might look and feel like.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: And I invite them to step into that world, to take a walk and to come back and describe it.

p3_items.justification: By holding space for participants to connect with a future of contentment and happiness, the practice fosters educated hope and highlights shared human longings for a better future.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: when you hold a space that feels safe for people to feel into what a future of contentment and happiness might feel like, then most people, actually, largely long for the same things.

post_title: Drucilla Cornell on the power of the public imagination

ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Take Back the Future
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Feminists, Muslim communities, and African-American families resisting state-sanctioned profiling and incarceration; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Drucilla Cornell and Ann Snitow
ostrom_summary.interactions: I7 self organizing activities: Reclaiming street spaces through performance arts and paper mâché dragons, marching alongside persecuted communities
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Expanded the definition of feminism and provoked community discussion

p1_justification: The practice 'Take Back the Future' represents a dynamic, critical process of social transformation that rejects static, mainstream definitions of political movements and actively seeks to dismantle dominant sociopolitical structures.
p1_verbatim_evidence: But that organisation, Take Back the Future, went completely against the grain of how feminism is being defined, how anti-racist politics are being defined...

p3_items.justification: The practice operates as a critical transformative approach that actively seeks to expand conscientization and political action beyond standard boundaries to include marginalized and dehumanized populations.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: We called a group called ‘Take Back the Future’ in which we expanded the definition of feminism to include anyone who was from below the bar of what was considered human.

p3_items.justification: The practice leverages artistic performance and symbolic visual elements to disrupt everyday routines, reclaiming public spaces to provoke dialogue and reflection.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: We would go with the dragon in front of rush hour train stations, and show what it meant to be eating the Constitution to provoke discussion. Rush hours and people running home would never be seen as a public space but we turned it into one.

p3_items.justification: The practice expresses a radical, liberatory hope by standing in active solidarity with targeted, marginalized communities to resist state oppression and systemic inequalities.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: We marched with Muslim communities who were being forced to re-register even though they had a Green card. We marched with African-American families whose sons were being told 'You get to go to war. You get to go to prison.' And we saw those as feminist issues.

practice_name: The Walk of What If workshop with international outdoor clothing company

ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Large international outdoor clothing company; GS5 operational rules: Two rules for the exercise: 1) not to be constrained by current budgets or development plans, 2) when someone suggested a What If question, participants must only respond with 'yes, and' (no 'yes, but').
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Representatives/employees of an international outdoor clothing company recognized for sustainability but seeking to act more urgently on the climate emergency.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I3 deliberation processes: Divided into groups of 6, walking in the mountains to brainstorm 'What If' questions about acting on the climate emergency using the 'yes, and' rule, then synthesizing these ideas into initiatives.

p1_justification: The practice employs an open-ended 'what if' framing to allow participants to dream up and debate multiple potential trajectories for their company, rejecting standard predictive planning.
p1_verbatim_evidence: The invitation was to come up with as many ‘What If’ questions they could in response to the overarching What If question “what if, in everything that it did, this company were to act as though this was a climate and ecological emergency?”
p2_justification: The workshop is a structured design and speculative exercise that uses physical walking, 'what if' scenario building, and generative rules to co-create initiatives.
p2_verbatim_evidence: Divided into groups of 6, walking in the mountains to brainstorm 'What If' questions about acting on the climate emergency using the 'yes, and' rule, then synthesizing these ideas into initiatives.

p3_items.justification: The workshop rules deliberately suspend real-world constraints to unlock radical brainstorming, encouraging social dreaming and ambitious visualization of alternative futures.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The first was that they were not to feel constrained by ‘What Is’, such as current budgets or development plans, rather to think big and bold and ambitious.

post_title: Some reflections on the difference between ‘Yes, but’ and ‘Yes, and’
practice_name: A Walk of What If (Patagonia Team Retreat Exercise)

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Outdoor clothing retail
ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Patagonia; GS5 operational rules: Two rules: participants must not feel constrained by existing policies, budgets, or organizational culture; and they must not respond with 'yes, but', only 'yes, and' to build on others' ideas.
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: groups of 6; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Staff from Patagonia; U6 norms social capital: Listening to and building on others' ideas using improv 'yes, and' principles.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Co-creating ideas for how Patagonia could act under a climate and ecological emergency.; I3 deliberation processes: A collaborative deliberation exercise called 'A Walk of What If'.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: High excitement, collective creativity, and generation of numerous What If questions.

p1_justification: The practice utilizes open-ended 'What If' prompts to explore a multiplicity of potential actions and paths Patagonia could take during an emergency, rather than focusing on a single predictive blueprint.
p1_verbatim_evidence: They went out in groups of 6, with the invitation to think of as many ideas for how Patagonia could act, in everything it does, as though this was a climate and ecological emergency.
p2_justification: The exercise constitutes a speculative methodology that uses structured 'what if' scenarios and improv rules to brainstorm and debate alternative futures for the organization.
p2_verbatim_evidence: we did an exercise called ‘A Walk of What If’. They went out in groups of 6, with the invitation to think of as many ideas for how Patagonia could act

p3_items.justification: The practice is designed to spark collaborative social dreaming and unlock imaginative capacity, explicitly instructing participants to ignore existing organizational constraints and build on others' ideas.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The first was that they were to not feel constrained by existing policies, budgets, organisational culture, rather to think in big, bold leaps. Secondly, they were not allowed to respond to What If questions with ‘yes, but’, only ‘yes, and’, to listen to and build on the ideas of others.

p1_justification: The practice of not mowing the grass represents a material, affirmative alternative to harsh, sterile urban environments, seeking to overcome alienation by reconnecting people with natural ecosystems.

p3_items.justification: The practice stimulates human curiosity and imagination, prompting participants to observe nature deeply and cognitively reimagine conventional urban aesthetic categories like 'tidy' and 'messy'.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: That time spent just observing them, in a way a child would observe a tiny forest, can greatly boost our imagination, our curiosity, our connection to and delight with the natural world.

post_title: A powerful tale of What If in action.

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Agriculture and community food cultivation.; RS3 size of resource system: Three community herb/berry beds, a 1-acre council site, and a 10-acre woodland allotment.; RS4 human constructed facilities: Two semi-derelict buildings on the council land planned for a permanent nursery and workshop space.; RS9 location: Lyme Regis, Dorset, UK
ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: Dorset Council and the local town council.; GS2 nongovernment organizations: Magna Housing association and the Woodland Trust.; GS4 property rights systems: Public land owned by Dorset Council leased to a third party, and woodland owned by the Woodland Trust.; GS5 operational rules: Using online Facebook coordination combined with offline mutual support of neighbors without computers.
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: Over 500 members.; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Local community members, including those with physical or mental health needs, off-line neighbors, and residents in social housing.; U4 location: Lyme Regis, Dorset, UK; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Rikey Austin (teddy hospital owner) and her geologist husband.; U6 norms social capital: Strong voluntary mutual aid and peer-to-peer solidarity, including donating excess produce and caring for neighbors.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Expertise in community gardening, composting, pruning, jam making, beekeeping, and water management.; U9 technology used: Facebook social media for organizing and No Dig gardening.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Organizing workshops and community skill-sharing calendars.; I5 investment activities: Selling their private residence to purchase land and establish a free community nursery.; I6 lobbying activities: Negotiating with a private leaseholder, Dorset Council, Magna Housing, and the Woodland Trust for land use.; I7 self organizing activities: Guerrilla gardening and volunteer-led community planting.; I8 networking activities: Partnering with local medical centers, housing associations, retirement homes, and regional trusts.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Weekly fresh food contributions to the local foodbank, and the integration of medical social prescribing.; O2 ecological performance measures: Production and distribution of thousands of seedlings and agricultural plants.

p1_justification: Lyme Garden Growers instantiates a concrete and affirmative utopian construct by establishing a physical, collaborative community asset—a free plant nursery and forest farm—to overcome social alienation and build a hopeful, localized alternative.
p1_verbatim_evidence: We’re in our fifties and I’d asked him to give up the house we’d spent fifteen years making our home... to set up a free community plant nursery.

p3_items.justification: The practice fosters educated hope and anticipatory consciousness by mobilizing collective local knowledge and setting up active skill-sharing workshops to build community resilience and mutual aid.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: People have also offered to share their knowledge of growing, pruning, composting jam making, canning, bee keeping, natural pest control, farmscaping, water management and vermicomposting so we’re setting up a classes and workshops calendar.

post_title: A powerful tale of What If in action.
location_string: Local church hall in Lyme Regis, UK

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Public community meeting and participatory visioning event.; RS3 size of resource system: One hundred spaces.; RS4 human constructed facilities: A local church hall.; RS9 location: Lyme Regis, Dorset, UK
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: One hundred people.; U4 location: Lyme Regis, Dorset, UK; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Rikey Austin, who organized the meeting and co-opted six local volunteers.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Utilizing an imaginative simulation of a localized future (wooden token currency, community shop, farmers market).; U9 technology used: Audio/visual performance cues (soundtrack, scripts, lights).
ostrom_summary.interactions: I3 deliberation processes: A four-minute experiential visioning exercise followed by an agricultural presentation by Charles Dowding.; I7 self organizing activities: Designing a customized interactive visioning script, sourcing soundscapes, and securing a free venue.; I8 networking activities: Inviting gardening expert Charles Dowding to provide a free educational talk as a draw for the community.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: A powerful emotional impact and high community engagement that set off a real transition movement in the town.

p1_justification: The practice instantiates an affirmative, concrete utopian vision of a localized community utilizing alternative currency and markets to overcome current socioeconomic realities.
p1_verbatim_evidence: I took them to an alternative Lyme Regis. I walked them down a high street with a farmers market where our excess fruit and veg could be bought with little wooden tokens.
p2_justification: The practice explicitly utilizes speculative storytelling, performance scripts, and audio design to immerse participants in an alternative future scenario.
p2_verbatim_evidence: I co-opted six people including the Baptist Minister and my son and wrote them a script. I created a soundtrack... used the ‘Time Machine’ exercise set out in the book at an event she organised.

p3_items.justification: The practice leverages imaginative simulation and sensory cues to unlock the participants' capacities for social dreaming, transporting them into another possible reality.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: One man told me that he didn't want to come back from that other Lyme... The feedback at the end was wonderful. People had been transported.

p3_items.justification: The experience of the speculative time machine fostered an active, forward-looking capability and community engagement that galvanized a real-world Transition movement.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: A powerful emotional impact and high community engagement that set off a real transition movement in the town.

p1_justification: Transition Town Totnes instantiates a concrete and affirmative utopian construct by establishing material, local alternatives such as a community currency, housing projects, and community-owned structures that seek to overcome existing systemic alienations.
p1_verbatim_evidence: We started a local currency, called the Totnes Pound, with the idea that it might enable more money to stay within the local economy... projects to build homes for local people that actually met local need... People started to take buildings into community ownership to operate them in service of the community.

p3_items.justification: The practice nurtures educated hope by rejecting both passive reliance on governmental action and individualistic resignation, positioning collective, community-led action as a viable path to a hopeful future.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: “If we wait for governments, it'll be too little, too late; if we act as individuals, it'll be too little; but if we act as communities, it might just be enough, just in time."

post_title: The Snowy Walk of What If

ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Patagonia 'Enviro' team; GS5 operational rules: Practicing 'Yes, And' rules where participants assume nothing is impossible, build on others' ideas without immediate reservations, and adopt a 'What If' frame of mind.; GS6 collective choice rules: Structuring brainstorming via small groups, writing proposals on A4 papers, clustering them along an urgency spectrum, and developing top ideas into detailed project proposals.
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: Around 25 leaders; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Leaders of Patagonia's corporate sustainability and environmental teams from across Europe.; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Facilitators Lucy, Herve, Mihela, Caroline, and Lionel, alongside author Rob Hopkins.; U6 norms social capital: A shared sense of safety, trust, unrushed time, and permission to use collective imagination without surveillance.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: An awareness of the climate crisis emergency requiring immediate 12-13% annual emissions cuts, and the use of 'What If' exercises to unlock imagination.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Sharing brainstormed proposals in a central circle and mapping them along an urgency spectrum.; I3 deliberation processes: A cooperative walkthrough of 'Walk of What If' in small groups, applying 'Yes, And' followed by intensive discussions around key questions: 'what would it look like?', 'what would we need to do?', 'what would we need to prune?', and 'what would make it bold and elegant?'.; I7 self organizing activities: Developing 5 detailed proposals for radical organizational shifts and debating changing the group name to the 'Climate Crisis' team.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Creation of 5 rounded, potentially deeply transformative project proposals for the organization.

p1_justification: The practice utilizes open-ended 'What If' scenarios to explore a multiplicity of potential futures and spark organizational shift discussions, rather than mapping to a deterministic path.
p1_verbatim_evidence: We had permission to ask what if, and to not squash that imagination with ‘yes but’... We were not under surveillance or imposed anxiety.
p2_justification: The practice explicitly implements a collaborative 'What If' walkthrough and uses design constraints (e.g., 'Yes, And' rules, framing questions) as design tools to debate and develop new alternatives.
p2_verbatim_evidence: Once in groups, we went off to discuss our idea with four key questions in mind, “what would it look like?”, “what would we need to do?”, “what would we need to prune”... and “what would make it bold and elegant?”

p3_items.justification: The workshop's design deliberately frees the participants' imaginative capacities through a safe space and structured brainstorming designed to promote social dreaming.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: We had permission to ask what if, and to not squash that imagination with ‘yes but’... We were not under surveillance or imposed anxiety.

p3_items.justification: The participants are explicitly driven to redesign organizational practices in response to the profound urgency of the climate and ecological emergency.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: leaders of the company’s Enviro teams from across Europe who are reflecting on how the company might play a more proactive and proportionate response to the climate and ecological emergency.

post_title: ‘Pop Up Tomorrow’ and a journey to 2030. A report.

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Imaginative community planning, future-modeling workshop, and participatory play; RS4 human constructed facilities: Battersea Arts Centre (BAC) Great Hall; RS9 location: Battersea, London
ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Encounters Arts and Transition Network; GS5 operational rules: Facilitated step-by-step group creative exercise, transitioning into 2030, planning topics, and physical model construction.
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: A packed hall of participants; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Diverse community participants, including local children, transition groups, and economic thinkers.; U3 history of use: Similar exercise previously held at the same room in 2012.; U4 location: London; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Lucy Neal and Ruth Ben Tovim (hosts/facilitators from Encounters Arts), Rob Hopkins (Town Centre Manager role); U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Using experiential play to create 'memories of the future' to combat pessimism.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Personal storytelling and writing reflections on blackboards and postcards; I3 deliberation processes: Group deliberations structured as local 'streets' and specific thematic development areas.; I7 self organizing activities: Co-building 3D models of town infrastructures using cardboard, willow, and craft materials.; I8 networking activities: Exploring and connecting built projects across the modeled town.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: High immersion, laughter, suspended disbelief, and powerful community engagement.

p1_justification: The practice treats the future not as a fixed blueprint, but as an open, pluralistic space for participants to co-create and explore alternative possibilities, connections, and opportunities.
p1_verbatim_evidence: explore potential connections, relationships, possibilities, opportunities. Some people stayed behind as hosts to explain what they had created, and others went out to network and connect.
p2_justification: The workshop uses creative writing (postcards from the future) and hands-on speculative design modeling to help participants construct and discuss alternative community infrastructures.
p2_verbatim_evidence: write onto postcards their reflections on the day, in the form of a postcard to themselves from the future... Once they had a site, then they set to work, building it in three dimensions using cardboard

p3_items.justification: The workshop actively builds 'memories of the future' as a learned, sensory capacity designed to generate hope and ward off feelings of despair, resignation, and pessimism.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: It gives people a deep, rich, multi-sensory imaginary picture of how the future could be, one that will hopefully be able to nudge in front of dystopias, pessimism, despair and resignation.

p3_items.justification: The practice relies on physical immersion and experiential play to spark participants' imaginations and prompt social dreaming about alternative value-driven worlds.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The hall filled with laughter and conversation, with people exploring what a world like this, modelled on these values and this creativity, might actually be like. To immerse themselves in it.

p3_items.justification: The practice engages with educators to advocate for active, transformative changes in schools, directly challenging the status quo of homework and high-stakes testing.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: I often speak to groups of public school educators and what I try to encourage there is instead of continuously moving in the direction of more and more schooling, more and more testing, let’s go in the other direction... Let’s do away with homework for elementary school.

post_title: Talking play and imagination with Peter Gray
practice_name: Long Island School District Morning Free Play Program

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS3 size of resource system: Seven elementary schools in the district; RS4 human constructed facilities: School building facilities including hallways, gym, art room, and outdoor playground; RS9 location: Long Island, New York
ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: The public school district governed by the district superintendent; GS2 nongovernment organizations: Let Grow (advised the superintendent on changes); GS5 operational rules: An hour of mixed-age free play with no adult interference unless safety is threatened; only basic rules are no destruction and no physical harm.; GS8 monitoring and sanctioning processes: Teachers act passively as lifeguards; older children naturally step in to resolve conflicts.
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: Students across seven elementary schools; 50% of parents opted out of testing; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Elementary school-age children; U4 location: Long Island, New York; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: The district school superintendent; U6 norms social capital: Children enforce rules themselves to protect their play time; teachers develop respect for kids' capability.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I3 deliberation processes: Deliberations between the author, organization, and school principals/superintendent; I4 conflicts among users: Minor conflicts and squabbles among playing children resolved by older children; I7 self organizing activities: Self-organized mixed-grade play
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Teachers gained respect for students; school environment lightened up; parents are highly supportive on social media.

p1_justification: The practice represents a pragmatic, real-world educational experiment that seeks to build a 'real utopia' within the existing public school framework by eliminating homework and establishing self-organized free play.
p1_verbatim_evidence: there’s a superintendent of schools on Long Island, New York, who has seven elementary schools in his district... He’s done away with homework in elementary school... He’s supported parents doing that... He’s increased recess.

p3_items.justification: The practice transforms the traditional, status-quo schooling model by actively dismantling conventional constraints like homework and testing in favor of child-led play.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: He’s done away with homework in elementary school... He’s supported parents doing that, so now half the parents in the school have opted their child out of standardised testing... instituted free play

p3_items.justification: The program emphasizes a pedagogical orientation that trusts in the inherent capability and plasticity of children to self-organize, cooperate, and resolve conflicts when freed from rigid adult control.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The teachers don’t have to do that. And they’re learning they don’t have to do it. If they let it go, the kids can solve the problems... The kids value this. They want it to occur and so they make sure that nobody is ruining it by fighting or by breaking something.

post_title: Why ‘Yes, and’ should be a vital part of any activist’s vocabulary
practice_name: 'From What Is to What If' Book Tour and Interactive Workshops

ostrom_summary.users: U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Rob Hopkins
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Interactive dreams of rapid transition and Q&A sessions during talks; I3 deliberation processes: Audience participation in the 'Yes, and' exercise; I4 conflicts among users: Audience skepticism and 'Yes, but' questions during Q&A
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Highly positive audience reception (standing ovations)

p1_justification: The practice focuses on exploring open-ended 'what if' possibilities and invites audiences to collectively dream of multiple open futures rather than presenting a predictive or deterministic roadmap.
p1_verbatim_evidence: speaking about my forthcoming book ‘From What Is to What If’... exploring with audiences, through interactive activities, that possibility that maybe, just maybe, we might be witnessing the beginning of a remarkable time of rapid transition.
p2_justification: The practice is structured as an interactive workshop methodology that uses sensory-rich storytelling, time travel imagination, and the 'Yes, and' exercise to debate alternative transition pathways.
p2_verbatim_evidence: exploring with audiences, through interactive activities, that possibility that maybe, just maybe, we might be witnessing the beginning of a remarkable time of rapid transition. I invite them to dream (literally)... I invite them to travel forward in time to see what that might sound, taste, smell and feel like.

p3_items.justification: The practice fosters active, forward-looking hope and works to overcome the audience's skeptical, defeatist, or cynical 'yes, but' assertions through constructive capability building.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: But every night, during Q&A, there are always several questions along the lines of “yes, but surely it’s too late/the people in charge won’t allow it/nothing ever really changes/it’s not possible”. And so in my talks now, when I get a ‘Yes, but’ question, I invite the audience to do the ‘Yes, and’ exercise.

p3_items.justification: The workshops actively engage participants in sensory-rich social dreaming and speculative imagination to visualize what a transformed future could feel like.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: I invite them to dream (literally)... I invite them to travel forward in time to see what that might sound, taste, smell and feel like.

post_title: ‘Totnes Declares: What Next?’: an act of community imagining.
practice_name: Totnes Declares a Climate and Ecological Emergency – What Next?

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Community planning, climate change response, and local ecological governance; RS4 human constructed facilities: Totnes Civic Hall; RS9 location: Totnes
ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: Totnes Town Council, district and county councils; GS2 nongovernment organizations: Transition Town Totnes, Extinction Rebellion Totnes & Area, Encounters Arts, Caring Town Totnes, Bridgetown Alive!, Fridays for Future; GS3 network structure: A collaborative coalition of local transition, activist, municipal, and community arts organizations; GS5 operational rules: Concentric circle introductions, 'milling' communication exercises, small facilitated table groups limited to 8 people, structured prompt rounds, and basic hand signals to aid flow.; GS6 collective choice rules: Consensus-oriented table feedback where members collectively agree on points that are considered 'good enough' to be written down.
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: A community gathering led by a content/design group of 10 people and supported by 15 discussion facilitators.; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Local transition activists, Extinction Rebellion members, elected representatives, local business owners, volunteers, food growers, and school strikers.; U4 location: Totnes (specifically Bridgetown, the town center, and outlying villages), plus visiting observers from Southampton, Paris, and California.; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Facilitated by Rob Hopkins and Ruth Ben-Tovim, with support from Jenny Gellatly, Rob Shorter, and a planning content group.; U6 norms social capital: Deep mutual respect, love for humanity and the planet, peace, appreciation, and community empowerment.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Awareness of climate science, ecological decline, and vulnerable resource sectors including local water, food, and farming.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Milling activities, structured provocations/presentations by diverse experts, and sharing personal reflections using symbolic objects.; I3 deliberation processes: Highly structured multi-round facilitated table discussions focused on what to keep, what to let go of, what is missing/needs to be created, and next steps for different spheres of influence.; I6 lobbying activities: Identifying actions for local decision makers (parish, town, district councils) and hosting local MP Dr. Sarah Wollaston as a listener.; I7 self organizing activities: Co-creating a physical cardboard book called 'Peoples Climate and Ecological Emergency Plan for Totnes'.; I8 networking activities: Bringing together diverse local environmental, youth, and political groups to foster network relationships.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Co-creation of a local emergency plan and deeply strengthened relationships among community members.; O3 externalities to other SESs: Spreading facilitation guides and insights to participants from other regions and countries to replicate the event design.

p1_justification: The practice focuses on building concrete, hopeful, and localized community alternatives to current climate realities, driven by mutual love, solidarity, and the co-creation of a local emergency action plan.
p1_verbatim_evidence: Together we are going to co–create the contents of a book called a Peoples Climate and Ecological Emergency Plan for Totnes... Rooted in love. We are all we need

p3_items.justification: The practice intentionally cultivates collective hope, love, and optimization to navigate climate anxiety and drive community-led transformation.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: options then included “been involved in Transition Town”, “been involved in XR”, “been an elected representative”, “felt despair”, “felt optimistic”... Let’s recall our love for the whole of humanity... Rooted in love. We are all we need

p3_items.justification: The workshop uses transformative pedagogy that moves beyond critiquing the current crisis to co-creating practical planning tools and directly influencing political structures.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Together we are going to co–create the contents of a book called a Peoples Climate and Ecological Emergency Plan for Totnes... For decision makers – parish, town, district council... to our MP Dr. Sarah Wollaston who attended the morning as a ‘keynote listener’

p3_items.justification: The event utilizes creative activities, symbolic objects, and physical materials to spark collaborative social dreaming and imaginative co-creation.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: visit one of two tables which were covered in different objects, and to choose one that represented how they were feeling... every table was given large sheets of cardboard and glue and scissors and invited to paste their ideas onto the page

p3_items.justification: The event uses the collective shock and despair of the ecological emergency as a pedagogical catalyst to disrupt the status quo and imagine what needs to be let go of and replaced.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Our town, district and county councils have now all declared climate emergencies... the purpose of the day was to convene a community conversation to begin the process of fleshing out what an appropriate response to the emergency might look like... Round Two... What doesn’t serve us anymore? What do we need to let go of?

post_title: Ben Goldfarb on how beavers can boost the collective imagination

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Aquatic ecosystems and wetlands; RS3 size of resource system: A single local reintroduction site; RS5 productivity of system: Extremely high capacity to generate biodiversity and support multiple dependent species when key ecological processes are restored.; RS9 location: Devon, United Kingdom
ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Environmental and rewilding groups promoting reintroduction, alongside angling groups actively opposing it.
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Anglers concerned about fish stocks, and land managers concerned about flooded fields and property damage.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Inaccurate biological beliefs regarding beaver diets (the myth that they eat fish) which shaped cultural opposition.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I4 conflicts among users: Friction between rewilding advocates and angling/land-owning interests over landscape changes.; I7 self organizing activities: Reintroducing beavers to a trial site to kickstart ecological restoration.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O2 ecological performance measures: Rapid and massive localized increases in biodiversity and indicator species (frog eggs, bats, and water beetles).; O3 externalities to other SESs: Developing policy, stakeholder, and engagement frameworks that will help guide and inform future reintroductions of other predator and mammal species.

p1_justification: The Devon Beaver Project represents a concrete, material, and hopeful alternative to ecological degradation, implementing active environmental rewilding to restore biodiversity and transform the landscape.
p1_verbatim_evidence: If the goal is to rewild the landscape, to bring back all of these creatures that have been lost... start with the animal that can support all of the other creatures. There’s this famous trial, the Devon Beaver project.

p3_items.justification: The project cultivates an active, anticipatory hope and learning capacity, demonstrating how successful coexistence with one reintroduced species can foster the capacity to imagine and implement wider future ecological restoration.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: beaver reintroduction is potentially going to inform the reintroduction of other creatures like Lynx and some day maybe wolves in the Scottish highlands. If you can learn to live with one kind of meddlesome animal that you’re returning, maybe that same framework can be applied to other meddlesome animals.

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Financial / Local Currency System; RS2 clarity of system boundaries: Limited to localized directory of shops in Totnes and excludes major chains; RS4 human constructed facilities: Printed physical paper notes and a mobile-phone based transaction system; RS9 location: Totnes, Devon
ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: Bank of England (informal legal oversight/regulation); GS2 nongovernment organizations: Transition Town Totnes (TTT); GS5 operational rules: Mutual consent between local merchants and consumers
ostrom_summary.users: U3 history of use: Inspired by an original 1810 Totnes Pound issued by the historical Bank of Totnes; U4 location: Totnes, Devon; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Rob Hopkins, Ben Brangwyn, Rick Lawrence, John Elford, and other Transition Town Totnes figures; U6 norms social capital: Encouraging face-to-face conversations, connection, and trust to counter isolation; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Economic localization frameworks and transition concepts; U9 technology used: Traditional paper printing with thermal security features, and digital mobile-phone banking
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Spurring local dialogue, media publicity, and cross-municipal learning; I7 self organizing activities: Grassroots community design, production, and rollout of a local medium of exchange; I8 networking activities: Networking with local currency groups in the UK, Belgium, and France
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Highly successful as a community arts and imagination spark, though failed to establish mass systemic market share; O3 externalities to other SESs: Inspired the establishment of many other local currencies across the UK and internationally

p1_justification: The Totnes Pound is conceptualized as a physical 'what if' question rolled out into the public space to spark collective imagination, representing an open speculative future rather than a rigid predictive model.
p1_verbatim_evidence: If we look at the Totnes Pound as being a community arts project, a ‘what if’ question that was rolled out into the town to spark the imagination... then I would say it has been a huge success.

p3_items.justification: The practice focuses on unlocking the imaginative capacities of the community and provoking dialogues and new connections through the physical prompt of the local currency.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: If we look at the Totnes Pound as being a community arts project, a ‘what if’ question that was rolled out into the town to spark the imagination... then I would say it has been a huge success.

post_title: Lucy Neal: “imagination is the most important thing in the whole wide world”

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Community art and sustainable transitions movement; RS9 location: Tooting, London
ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Transition Town Tooting; GS3 network structure: Part of the wider Transition movement
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: People across different ages and cultures in the neighborhood; U4 location: Tooting, London; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Lucy Neal; U6 norms social capital: Creativity, community imagination, playfulness; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Systemic collapses at all system levels, responding with imaginative frameworks
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Engaging community members with provocative questions on blackboards outside of local pop-up shops; I3 deliberation processes: Creating spaces to ask creative "what if" questions; I7 self organizing activities: Organizing creative projects, pop-up shops, and community events
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Affirming citizens in their community and facilitating interpersonal connections

p1_justification: The practice centers on establishing open-ended 'what if' spaces and inviting diverse residents to imagine different futures without prescribing a singular blueprint.
p1_verbatim_evidence: One of the things that it feels like you and Ruth Ben-Tovim and Encounters do so beautifully is creating those ‘what if’ spaces and asking ‘what if’ really beautifully.

p3_items.justification: The practice focuses on unlocking the community's imagination through local pop-up shops, creative prompts, and open questions to spark social dreaming.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: outside the shop we put a big blackboard and it said ‘What is the spirit of Tooting?’ and other days it said ‘What makes you smile?’ or ‘Who or what do you miss?’

p3_items.justification: The realization of systemic collapses at all levels acts as a critical catalyst, prompting a deep reconsideration of the role of imagination in responding to these crises.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: absorbed information in greater detail and depth about quite how systemic the collapses that we were undergoing at every sort of systems level. Therefore I began to very deeply question what a role could be for somebody that worked with the imagination

ostrom_summary.users: U4 location: Tooting; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Lucy Neal; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Reimagining street infrastructure and lacking public green spaces
ostrom_summary.interactions: I6 lobbying activities: Negotiating with municipal transport authorities to relocate buses for a day; I7 self organizing activities: Pop-up installation of a village green in a transit circle
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Demonstrating the viability of a new communal space to local skeptics

p1_justification: The practice is structured as an open-ended 'what if' scenario that temporarily transforms a familiar transit circle into a village green to explore alternative possibilities and stimulate community dialogue.
p1_verbatim_evidence: What if we created a pop-up village green there?

p3_items.justification: The project uses 'what if' questioning as a mode of social dreaming, inviting community members to cognitively re-evaluate a highly familiar public space in a completely different way.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: That was a good example of an invitation to people to do something differently in a place that they were very, very familiar with.

p3_items.justification: The intervention seeks to counteract local skepticism by physically demonstrating that a different, communal future is achievable, fostering an active belief in alternative possibilities.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: We’ve played with the idea of what could be possible but let nobody say after today that it’s not possible.

post_title: Lucy Neal: “imagination is the most important thing in the whole wide world”

ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: 350 people; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Ruth Ben-Tovim; U6 norms social capital: Collective play, shared making purpose, and suspension of disbelief; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Memory of the future, practicing scenario-based community options
ostrom_summary.interactions: I3 deliberation processes: Step-by-step participatory framing from individual stories to town scale; I7 self organizing activities: Imaginative micro-collaborations like creating a joint brewery and bakery
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Inspirational and emotional impact that eventually seeded real-world enterprises

p1_justification: The practice is framed as a playful simulation that explores open-ended community alternatives, helping participants rehearse various potential futures rather than locking them into a single plan.
p1_verbatim_evidence: when you play and open up different possibilities, you’re rehearsing what sort of ideas of what could happen.
p2_justification: The practice is a structured, participatory workshop that uses storytelling scales and collaborative making to design and build a simulated speculative town.
p2_verbatim_evidence: Ruth framed the levels of story of yourself and your neighbourhood and your street and your town, so it didn’t go straight to “How are you going to rebuild your town?” but it worked.

p3_items.justification: The workshop fosters an active 'memory of the future' that serves as a hopeful, anticipatory force, eventually inspiring participants to establish real-world enterprises.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Inspirational and emotional impact that eventually seeded real-world enterprises

p3_items.justification: The methodology leverages collective play and a shared making purpose to unlock creative potential and explore new ideas of what could be.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: people suddenly found themselves very productively and creatively with a lot of other people. And they didn’t know everyone in the room and yet they had a shared making purpose.

p1_verbatim_evidence: to build a raked seating in the River Thames or to light a flame out of Bankside Power Station... you realise that every single dynamic around that place and how people connected to it, and how they remembered it, and how they carried the experience, that there had been a renewal in how that place was then going to be remembered

ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: The Centre for Story-Based Strategy; GS5 operational rules: Provides day-long, week-long advanced training, and online programs to offer strategic support to social change partners.
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Frontline communities at the intersection of poverty, pollution, and racism, focusing on climate, economic, and social justice.; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Co-Directors Shana McDavis-Conway and Bernice Julie Shaw; U9 technology used: Digital organizing tools, video, animation, and visuals
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Conducting trainings and online training programs to help groups build strategic stories; I3 deliberation processes: Sharing personal stories and experiences of inspiration and hurt in dedicated creative spaces; I7 self organizing activities: Helping groups construct strategic narratives and campaign messaging

p1_justification: The practice instantiates Concrete and Affirmative Utopianism by grounding its visionary work in the material struggles of frontline communities to achieve affirmative transformations in climate, economic, and social justice.
p1_verbatim_evidence: They particularly work with groups in frontline communities that are at the intersection of poverty, pollution and racism, with a focus on climate, economic and social justice.

p3_items.justification: The practice explicitly mobilizes radical imagination and hopeful storytelling to empower marginalized frontline communities in their fight against systemic inequalities.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The Centre for Story-Based Strategy ('where imagination builds power') are pioneers in this field.

p3_items.justification: The practice employs active, transformative pedagogy through structured story-based trainings that support organizations in translating their narratives into direct political action and campaigns.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: We spend a lot of our time providing trainings for social change organisations. We try to help groups... help them shape their vision, their overarching story for their work. Sometimes it’s about a specific campaign... how do we translate that story into specific campaign messaging

p3_items.justification: The practice leverages digital tools, animation, and storytelling to unlock the imaginative capacity of communities, allowing them to visualize and project alternative narratives.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: especially because of digital organising, there’s really been opportunities to do storytelling work in different ways. There’s access to technology for communities that previously would not have had the ability to create their own video, their own animation, their own visuals

p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Our pedagogical ethos aims to develop creative thinking not as a subject silo... but as a mode of inquiry, and as inquiry-led, practice-based learning in all fields

ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: Danish government / certified consultant; GS4 property rights systems: Private 'Free School'; GS5 operational rules: Guidelines for learning goals, project-based interdisciplinary curriculum with no national tests, and a strict no-phones policy.; GS7 constitutional rules: Free school regulations and requirements to receive government funding; GS8 monitoring and sanctioning processes: Annual assessment by a government-certified consultant
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: 150 children, ages 5 to 14; U3 history of use: Operating since 2014, started with 43 children.; U4 location: Copenhagen, Denmark; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Karen MacLean and her co-founding friend; U6 norms social capital: Values of imagination, Transition, and sustainability; relating to knowledge in a way that involves children in Transition.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Preparing and exciting children to participate in Transition, anchoring learning in sensory experiences of the local environment.; U9 technology used: Technology treated as tools, including coding instruction and computers in a lab.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Open-ended interdisciplinary project-based learning starting with sensory experiences.; I5 investment activities: Clay plastering classrooms, sustainable building and restoration.; I7 self organizing activities: Founding of the school by local parents.; I8 networking activities: Weekly field trips to forests, beaches, museums, or businesses.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Fostering creativity and imagination through open-ended learning products without fixed correct answers.

p1_justification: The practice is a green free school established as a material, real-world alternative that values sustainability and Transition, striving to actively engage children in creating a hopeful sociopolitical alternative.
p1_verbatim_evidence: They are a school that values imagination, Transition and sustainability above everything else... we decided that we would start a school for our daughters to teach them the things, the skills, the knowledge and the way of relating to knowledge that would start from the very beginning involving them in Transition.

p3_items.justification: The school adopts a transformative pedagogical orientation that prepares children to actively engage in the hands-on, systemic ecological and social Transition movement.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: I became more and more curious to know what a school would look like that actually prepared children to participate in creating Transition, and who would also want to...

p3_items.justification: The curriculum rejects rigid, pre-defined 'right answers' to open up space for creative play, sensory exploration, and social dreaming among the students.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Being open about the product of learning, the outward sign of learning, and not having a right answer, is incredibly important in my mind to fostering creativity and to letting the imagination come into play.

p3_items.justification: The pedagogy seeks to transform students from passive consumers of technology into active producers and self-determined subjects.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: We try to teach the children to use them for their own purposes, and in order to teach them to be not consumers and users, but producers...

p3_items.justification: By withholding WiFi, the practice disrupts and unsettles the dominant, deterministic habits of constant digital connection that participants take for granted.
