post_title: Stella Duffy on Fun Palaces and “saying Yes to everything”

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Culture, arts, science, craft, tech, digital, heritage, and sport; RS4 human constructed facilities: Public spaces, buildings, and local venues (sometimes requiring temporary marquees/tents).; RS9 location: Britain
ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Fun Palaces (campaign organization co-directed by Stella Duffy).; GS3 network structure: A highly lean, decentralized national support team linking and facilitating independent local groups.; GS5 operational rules: Events must be free, take place on the specified weekend, and creators must fill out a registration form on the website agreeing to terms and conditions.; GS6 collective choice rules: Structured around Open Space Technology and hui principles, allowing participants to self-sort and self-govern based on passion.
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Highly inclusive demographics; a significant portion of makers include ethnic minorities, disabled individuals, and people from outside the mainstream cultural elite.; U3 history of use: 5 years of operation at the time of writing.; U4 location: Hyper-local geographical communities across Britain.; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Co-directors (Stella Duffy) acting as facilitators, alongside decentralized 'lead makers' within communities.; U6 norms social capital: Motto of 'everyone an artist, everyone a scientist' and 'the genius in everyone'; built on radical trust, saying yes, and handing over power.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Focus on experiential and hands-on learning through directly creating culture rather than acting as a passive audience.; U9 technology used: Centralized website for registration, terms agreement, and publicity.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Amplifying and sharing local grassroots work nationally; linking groups to share resources/support.; I5 investment activities: Sourced with extremely minimal or zero additional funding, relying heavily on local sponsorship and mutual aid.; I7 self organizing activities: Empowering local citizens to step up, make decisions, and self-organize their own local programs without top-down criteria.; I8 networking activities: Linking grassroots community groups with larger cultural institutions.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: High quality of community engagement and self-determined standard of excellence.

p1_justification: The practice embodies an active, affirmative alternative to contemporary societal alienation by enabling local, everyday citizens to actively contribute to and shape their own cultural environments.
p1_verbatim_evidence: Often in Britain when people talk about ‘community’ they mean ethnic communities or family communities, but we’re aware that most people don’t get to contribute to where they live, and Fun Palaces supports local people to be as creative as they would like to be

p3_items.justification: The practice employs an active pedagogy that transitions individuals from passive cultural consumers to active creators, directly learning through the hands-on process of organizing their own programs.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: What Fun Palaces is about is supporting people into being creators... People learn from the experiential practice of creating their own Fun Palace.

p3_items.justification: The practice frames its approach around the rejection of the elitist notion of exclusive 'cultural leaders,' asserting instead the universal capacity and creative potential inherent in all human beings.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: we genuinely believe in our motto, “everyone an artist, everyone a scientist”, and “the genius in everyone”... our real emphasis is on supporting people who may not consider themselves cultural leaders

post_title: When imagination meets Brexit: the story of the Totnes Passport

ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: An informal group of citizens/friends led by Jonathan Cooper; GS5 operational rules: Passports are given free of charge to individuals who attend an event and read the Oath of Allegiance; GS6 collective choice rules: Informal consultation with the public/peers on the rules and wording of the oath; GS7 constitutional rules: The Oath of Allegiance, which lays down core values of the project and pledges EU citizenship maintenance
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: Over 500 passport holders; U4 location: Totnes, Devon, UK; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Jonathan Cooper; U6 norms social capital: Commitment to compassion, endeavour, ingenuity, altruism, diligence, daring, and peace; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Advocacy for decentralized city states in a federation as a successful model of governance; U9 technology used: Facebook and Twitter
ostrom_summary.interactions: I3 deliberation processes: Drafting and sending the oath around for community feedback; I4 conflicts among users: Complete absence of hostility or trolling from the public; I5 investment activities: Funding the passport production costs and organizing donation channels to recoup costs or support the People's Vote campaign; I6 lobbying activities: Tweeting Michel Barnier to ask for recognition of Totnes as an independent city state within the EU; I7 self organizing activities: Conducting physical passport distribution sessions at the Totnes Guildhall; I8 networking activities: Promoting the concept to other UK cities and towns to encourage similar initiatives
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Providing a playful, humorous platform for protest that defuses tension

p1_justification: The practice instantiates an affirmative, value-driven alternative to post-Brexit isolationism by establishing a symbolic city-state that actively asserts ongoing alignment with European ideals.

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Market gardening, woodworking, pottery, charcoal production, and compost production.; RS2 clarity of system boundaries: Clearly demarcated physical site visible from the road containing polytunnels, beds, a cob wall, and a shop.; RS4 human constructed facilities: Polytunnels, a cob wall with roundwood pole roof, vegetable beds, and a roadside shop.; RS9 location: South Devon
ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: The prison system and criminal justice system, which refers day-release and at-risk candidates.; GS2 nongovernment organizations: Independent charity; GS3 network structure: Informal support and food donation network with Schumacher College.; GS5 operational rules: Structured workday combining manual crafts with daily communal cooking and eating.
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: 64 participants over 5 years.; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Individuals in prison or at risk of prison, often with mental health issues, drug addiction, trauma, and poverty.; U3 history of use: Up to 9 months on-site per participant.; U4 location: South Devon; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Chris Parsons, project founder and manager.; U6 norms social capital: Rebuilding trust, cultivating a non-criminal identity, non-judgmental community support, and shared vulnerability.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: A custom Theory of Change built around trust and hope.; U8 importance of resource: A critical 'halfway house' providing safety, stability, skills, and community integration to transition out of institutionalization.; U9 technology used: Basic hand tools, art supplies, and craft workshop machinery.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Story swapping during shared, equalizing communal lunches.; I4 conflicts among users: Early defensiveness/denial shifting over time to trust and admitting past harms.; I7 self organizing activities: Tapering long-term contact and support after participants leave the program.; I8 networking activities: Inviting outside community members, judges, and cooks to join communal meals.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: A 4% reoffending rate, which is significantly below the national average.; O3 externalities to other SESs: Successful resettlement and community integration for former prisoners.

p1_justification: The practice functions as a concrete, material, and affirmative alternative to the carceral system, designed to overcome alienation and foster a supportive, integrated community.
p1_verbatim_evidence: There had to be something between prison, and the real world... providing a supported route back into employment and community for those in prison or at risk of going to prison

p3_items.justification: The project is driven by a theory of change focused on trust and mutual belief, cultivating active hope in a better future for marginalized individuals.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: There is a Theory of Change that exists here. Again, it’s quite simple. It is broadly built around trust, and someone believing in you.

p3_items.justification: The pedagogical approach assumes the malleability and potential of human nature, helping individuals to move past fixed criminal labels and actively transform their identities.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Then as time moves on, you start to get to well actually admitting, and they want to admit that they did wrong. It’s at that point that things can change... very much encourage people to form a non-criminal identity here

p3_items.justification: The campaign demonstrates educated hope by converting the deep emotional impact of the book into active, self-organized efforts to transform children's educational environments.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: It was begun by one woman in Scotland, Jane Beaton, a bus driver from Midlothian, who looked at the book, and tweeted me, and said, “This has just knocked me over. I want to get a copy into every school in Scotland.”

p3_items.justification: The practice leverages radical imagination and hopeful storytelling to foster community connection, inspiring participants to generously donate their time and energy to support a shared vision of ecological and social flourishing.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The generosity and the gift – something you must be very familiar with – this giving of time, of energy, of passion, has been unbelievable.

p1_justification: The practice is a performance of 'Urinetown', a cautionary future vision concerning water scarcity and societal control that utilizes Brechtian theater to prompt critical reflection on environmental and societal futures, featuring characters that drive the story's resistance.
p1_verbatim_evidence: using a Brechtian theatrical style to prompt reflection on environmental and societal futures... In particular, Djavan van de Fliert and Zoë Sleath who play the lead characters Bobby Strong and Hope Cladwell, are amazing.

p3_items.justification: The theatrical production acts as a dystopian warning about resource depletion, successfully instilling a conscious urge in the audience to conserve water in their daily lives.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Go see it, you’ll never leave the tap running while you clean your teeth again.

p3_items.justification: The production uses a Brechtian style to create intellectual distancing, prompting the audience to actively analyze and think about the issues rather than experiencing the performance as passive, purely emotional consumers.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: The play is presented in the Brechtian style, with two characters... regularly stepping out of the flow of the story to tell the audience what is about to happen, and getting them thinking about what’s happening rather than just being passive consumers.

post_title: Hilary Powell and Dan Edelstyn on ‘The Bank Job’: “People seemed starved of making”

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Art-led complementary currency creation and debt-abolition system.; RS2 clarity of system boundaries: Targeting debt specifically within the E17 postcode of Walthamstow.; RS3 size of resource system: A total of 13,700 currency notes, representing £50,000 worth of value.; RS4 human constructed facilities: A former bank building rented from co-working organization IndyCube.; RS9 location: Walthamstow, London
ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: Waltham Forest Council; GS2 nongovernment organizations: IndyCube, Lipman-Miliband Foundation, Raymond Williams Foundation, and UCL Urban Laboratory.; GS5 operational rules: A strict policy of paying London Living Wage to all print production line participants.
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Local people of diverse backgrounds, from printmakers/artists to those with zero previous experience.; U4 location: Walthamstow, London; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Artist Hilary Powell and filmmaker Dan Edelstyn.; U6 norms social capital: Strong local community and grassroots networks built over a 10-year period of local residency.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Economic and monetary reform frameworks (like Creditocracy and Debt history) maintained in a community library.; U9 technology used: Traditional letterpress, screen printing, etching, and foil-blocking machinery.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Conducting evening discussions and public events on economic education, cryptocurrencies, and currency alternatives.; I4 conflicts among users: Debating local skepticism and deeply-held moral taboos surrounding the concept of debt and debt abolition.; I5 investment activities: Personal financial risks/savings alongside limited municipal and institutional grants, resulting in personal deficit.; I7 self organizing activities: Organizing a printing production line, designing the currency, renting and establishing the bank headquarters, and organizing community heists.; I8 networking activities: Connecting with local designers, neighbors, photographers, local charitable actors, and American activists from Strike Debt.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Raised over £15,000 towards buying back debt and funding local causes.; O3 externalities to other SESs: Sought after as a blueprint for replication in other towns and inspired local school children to build symbolic banknotes.

p1_justification: The practice instantiates a concrete and material alternative to debt-based capitalism by establishing a physical bank that prints its own currency to raise funds and actively buy back and abolish local debt.
p1_verbatim_evidence: The idea of the Bank is that rather than getting people into debt, it buys up their debts, and abolishes them. We’re printing our own money in there, and the money that we make from selling the money, goes into abolishing debt

p3_items.justification: The practice employs active community education, discussion events, and a shared library of radical economic literature to foster critical consciousness and conscientization regarding monetary reform.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: We had eight events that looked at things from other money, like other money creation, to cryptocurrencies, to economic education... continue it as a hub of economic conversation

p3_items.justification: The practice uses the imaginative provocation of printing physical alternative currency and staging 'bank jobs' to break through moral taboos, spark public arguments, and inspire children to imagine alternative ways of helping the poor.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: parents were coming up to say their child had locked themselves in the room and come out with a whole set of bank notes that they’d made for poor people!

post_title: A report from #CTRLshift2018

ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: around 150 people; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: diverse organisations, a mixture of grizzled battle-scarred community/sustainability veterans and younger, more idealistic activists, and people with very varied degrees of anger/exclusion/distress; U4 location: Across the UK and further afield; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Andy Goldring, Jay Tompt, Jez Hall, Zarina Ahmad, Lucy Antal, Caroline Lucas, Hilary Wainwright
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: sharing the successes and potential of different movements; I3 deliberation processes: Home Groups, Open Space, Fishbowl, workshops, Design Charettes; I4 conflicts among users: Struggles to move away from key peeves, soap box issues, and vague generalisations; barriers to younger women speaking up; I7 self organizing activities: Design Charettes resulting in worked-up proposals; I8 networking activities: stepping out of silos, building coalitions, and deepening relationships
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Lots of great connections and life-affirming interaction

p1_justification: The practice focuses on sharing and scaling real, material alternatives to current systems, such as permaculture, community ownership, and the solidarity economy, aiming to build holistic, hopeful alternative realities.
p1_verbatim_evidence: The aim of the event was to share the successes and potential of the different movements, from permaculture to community ownership, from the solidarity economy to rethinking governance

p3_items.justification: The practice fosters a supportive environment of 'educated hope' and life-affirming interaction among idealistic activists and sustainability veterans, countering distress about the current state of the world.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: I made lots of great connections... Spending time with so many what one person called “positive deviants” is wonderful, and very life-affirming

p3_items.justification: The event employs active facilitation techniques and collaborative design processes to move participants beyond passive critique or general complaints toward active social planning and the co-creation of concrete proposals.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: It was fascinating how hard it was to coax people away from their key peeves, their soap box issues... hard it was to get some people to move away from vague generalisations... groups formed around each one, and we had just over an hour working in ‘Design Charettes’... to come up with a worked up, beautifully presented proposal.

post_title: Sarah Woods on imagination and “the crisis of what comes next”.

ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: 200 people; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: People from Birmingham; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Sarah Woods
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Sharing personal stories which form the base of the opera; I3 deliberation processes: Creating a dialogue to understand opposite positions and find collective solutions
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Participants feel energized, positive, and linked to wider systemic issues

p1_justification: The practice builds a pluralistic space through a 'story system' that allows participants to map opposite positions and visualize multiple solutions, rejecting single predictive pathways.
p1_verbatim_evidence: Get people to tell each other those stories, and to understand that there are a number of different positions, and even opposite positions. If that dialogue is really well held... the idea is that you get people to realise there are a lot of different positions. You start to create a story system, and within that story system, people start to see that there are also then a number of different solutions.

p3_items.justification: The practice emphasizes transforming positive collective energy and dialogue into active agency and conscientization regarding large-scale systemic problems.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: It’s very energising for people to work together and talk together, and from that you can start to create action, and when people are feeling energised and positive, you can also get them to see how it links to the biggest problems that we’ve got.

p3_items.justification: The practice leverages storytelling and the collaborative creation of a narrative environment to provoke debate and enable participants to visualize multiple alternatives and solutions.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Get people to tell each other those stories, and to understand that there are a number of different positions, and even opposite positions. If that dialogue is really well held... the idea is that you get people to realise there are a lot of different positions. You start to create a story system, and within that story system, people start to see that there are also then a number of different solutions.

post_title: Sarah Woods on imagination and “the crisis of what comes next”.

ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Car enthusiasts, people whose lives are centered around their cars; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Belief that car transport models will change in the next 10 years, coupled with an uncertainty on what will replace it
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Interviews on memories of car ownership

p1_justification: The practice explores future possibilities of transport in an open-ended, non-predictive manner by asking participants to imagine a future where they no longer drive in the same way, without providing a definitive blueprint.
p1_verbatim_evidence: I said to everybody at the end of the interview, “Do you think in another ten years you’ll still be driving around in the same ways as you are now, in your car?” And they said, “No. No, no, I won’t.” And I said, “So what will you be doing instead?” And they said, “I don’t know.”

p3_items.justification: The interviews act as an imaginative provocation, prompting car enthusiasts to reflect on alternative futures and what they will do once their current car-centric lifestyles inevitably change.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: I said to everybody at the end of the interview, “Do you think in another ten years you’ll still be driving around in the same ways as you are now, in your car?” And they said, “No. No, no, I won’t.” And I said, “So what will you be doing instead?” And they said, “I don’t know.”

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Agri-food / craft brewing; RS3 size of resource system: Small-scale brewing facility, brewing once a day, five days a week with a 600-litre capacity start; RS4 human constructed facilities: A physical brewing facility set up in railway arches; RS9 location: Druid Street, Bermondsey, London
ostrom_summary.governance: GS5 operational rules: A flat management system where tasks are highly rotated among all employees, and work shifts are synchronized to a single common rhythm.; GS6 collective choice rules: Individual brewers on duty are given autonomy over choose hops and stylistic details on their brewing day rather than sticking to a rigid preconceived recipe template.
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: 14 workers; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Employees are well-compensated and express high job satisfaction; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Evin O'Riordan (founder); U6 norms social capital: High level of mutual trust, deep familiarity, and mutual understanding that avoids internal division between departments; U9 technology used: A 600-litre brewing kit, forklift, bottling line, and kegging equipment
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Employees maintain close horizontal communication so that everyone knows how to run all elements of the business; I4 conflicts among users: Overcrowding and high tourist volume at their Saturday taproom caused friction with neighboring businesses and compromised the brewery's focus, leading to its closure
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: A resilient, non-compartmentalized, and healthy workplace environment with exceptionally low employee turnover.

p1_justification: The practice instantiates an affirmative, holistic alternative to alienated labor through a flat management and task-rotation system that fosters trust and shared responsibility.
p1_verbatim_evidence: our system is relatively flat which means that the responsibility is shared between all of us. Like I said, we all start at the same time, finish at the same time, so you have that kind of thing of a common rhythm. It’s not compartmentalised... We all take turns brewing... We all take turns bottling. We all take turns driving the forklift.

p1_justification: The practice frames a complete societal collapse and loss of electricity as a catalytic rupture that demands participants entirely rewire and reconstruct human systems.
p1_verbatim_evidence: Okay, it’s the zombie apocalypse. We’ve lost all electricity. You have 48 hours to find a way to reconnect and rewire humanity so we can save ourselves
p2_justification: The practice uses imaginative 'what if' narratives, such as a zombie apocalypse scenario, within collaborative design sessions to generate practical off-grid technologies.
p2_verbatim_evidence: instead of saying, “Oh, how can we save electricity?”, they say, “Okay, it’s the zombie apocalypse. We’ve lost all electricity. You have 48 hours to find a way to reconnect and rewire humanity so we can save ourselves”. And then the ideas they come up with, they can then take into third world places

p3_items.justification: The practice utilizes playful speculative scenarios as a tool for imaginative provocation to unlock creative engineering capacities in digital native participants.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: instead of saying, “Oh, how can we save electricity?”, they say, “Okay, it’s the zombie apocalypse. We’ve lost all electricity. You have 48 hours to find a way to reconnect and rewire humanity so we can save ourselves”.

p3_items.justification: The design sessions leverage the fictional crisis of a zombie apocalypse and loss of power as a rapid psychological catalyst to compel participants to redesign survival infrastructure.

post_title: Hannah Fox on how a community’s imagination reshaped a museum.
practice_name: Derby Silk Mill / Museum of Making

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Cultural heritage, museums, and community workshop spaces; RS2 clarity of system boundaries: The physical building and grounds of the Silk Mill Museum; RS4 human constructed facilities: Silk Mill building, workshop space with CNC machine, laser cutters, movable café, and chairs.; RS8 storage characteristics: Open storage display planned for 130,000 collections items.; RS9 location: Derby
ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: Derby City Council, Heritage Lottery Fund, Arts Council; GS2 nongovernment organizations: An independent trust established to run Derby's museums.; GS3 network structure: An extensive co-production network involving architects, designers, staff, and volunteers.; GS4 property rights systems: Public ownership / public resources; GS5 operational rules: Human-centred design thinking methodology; GS6 collective choice rules: Co-production, collaborative prototyping, and shared ownership
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: 30,000 people; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Volunteers, local bands, young people, elderly people, and local citizens of Derby.; U3 history of use: Long-term community attachment to the local museum.; U4 location: Derby locality; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Hannah Fox (Facilitator / Pioneer); U6 norms social capital: Psychological safety, tolerance for failure, transparency, and collective action.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Human-centred design mind-sets.; U8 importance of resource: High level of passion and emotional investment.; U9 technology used: Digital manufacturing tools (CNC machines, laser cutters).
ostrom_summary.interactions: I1 harvesting levels of diverse users: Increasing public access to museum assets from 4% to 100%.; I2 information sharing among users: Crowd-sourcing city expertise and co-designing elements.; I3 deliberation processes: Events, prototyping workshops, design teams, and public consultations.; I4 conflicts among users: Public anger regarding museum closure and active resistance to institutional change.; I5 investment activities: Capital fundraising and volunteer labor contribution.; I7 self organizing activities: Co-making the museum's interior fit-out by the public.; I8 networking activities: Collaboration with Rolls Royce and local schools.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Secured major £16.4m funding and engaged 30,000 participants in iterative design.

p1_justification: The practice utilizes open-ended design thinking mindsets to encourage citizens to imagine and co-produce a shared vision of what a public museum can be, rather than implementing a rigid, pre-determined plan.
p1_verbatim_evidence: We use design thinking approaches, so human centred design mind-sets to define and understand, to think and imagine what might be

p3_items.justification: The design thinking and prototyping workshops focus on unlocking the public's imagination to visualize and experiment with alternative futures for the museum space.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: We use design thinking approaches, so human centred design mind-sets to define and understand, to think and imagine what might be, and then to model and prototype that

p3_items.justification: The process transforms citizens and volunteers into active makers and shapers of their local museum, shifting them from passive consumers of heritage to active subjects who co-produce the space.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: the expectations of the people that it’s not just us doing it, but we are doing it, together, for our collective good and for the good of our city, and our citizens

post_title: George Monbiot on how boarding school destroys the imagination

ostrom_summary.governance: GS5 operational rules: Extreme levels of discipline, including mandatory cold showers, early morning runs, and systematic book censorship.; GS8 monitoring and sanctioning processes: Physical punishment (merciless caning/flogging) and confiscation of literature deemed unsuitable.
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: Small boys and staff; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Wealthy upper-class elite, isolated from local and working-class culture.; U4 location: On-site boarding dormitories; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Draconian headmasters exercising iron rule, often lost or depressive figures.; U6 norms social capital: An extremist conforming system that completely breaks local/family attachments to reattach pupils to Empire, army, and civil service.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Entirely backward-looking mental models reflecting peak colonial and Edwardian class structures, with zero knowledge of how the wider public lives.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I4 conflicts among users: Pupils being 'at war' with the system, forcing a polarization of complete acceptance or complete rejection.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Boarding School Syndrome, characterized by repressed trauma, inability to connect emotionally with others, and a severely limited or excised imagination.; O3 externalities to other SESs: Produces political leaders who lack imagination, are completely institutionalized, and fail to understand poverty or the public.

p1_justification: The practice conceptualizes the historical British boarding school system as a realized dystopian environment of extreme control, isolation, and brutality, treating it as an empirical historical reality rather than a speculative warning.
p1_verbatim_evidence: Everywhere else in Britain these people are regarded as war criminals. In this school they were neither forgotten nor processed.

p3_items.justification: The boarding school experience instills deep-seated, existential trauma and constant vigilance in children, producing long-term psychological damage and emotional repression.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Boarding School Syndrome, characterized by repressed trauma, inability to connect emotionally with others, and a severely limited or excised imagination.

post_title: Talking imagination and participation with JR
location_string: La Belle de Mai, Marseille, France

ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Multicultural neighborhood with ethnic tensions, featuring isolated older residents and local youth; U6 norms social capital: Lack of connection and mutual trust prior to project; creation of strong intergenerational connection through the project; U9 technology used: Archival images enlarged and pasted on public neighborhood walls
ostrom_summary.interactions: I3 deliberation processes: Dialogue between youth and local elder, sharing oral history triggered by the artwork; I7 self organizing activities: Sourcing historical/personal photographs directly from community members to paste publicly
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Generated intergenerational and intercultural connection, reducing neighborhood isolation

p1_justification: The practice represents an affirmative sociopolitical transformation in a fragmented neighborhood, actively working to overcome isolation and alienation through community-driven art.
p1_verbatim_evidence: there was an old French man that lived there and didn’t have that many friends because he lost the way to connect with the youth... I remember him at the end of the day telling me, “I’ve never talked so much with the people around me in this neighbourhood. I’ve never had a connection with them.”

p3_items.justification: The practice uses public art to displace personal and historical images into new contexts, unlocking different meanings that provoke curiosity, storytelling, and reflection on community values.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: It’s amazing to see sometimes what just a little bit of paper, a little bit of photography and image, sometimes enlarged a little, but replaced in a different context, can create a whole different meaning.

post_title: Talking imagination and participation with JR
location_string: A forgotten suburb neighborhood of Paris, France

ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: The local Mayor and the French President; GS5 operational rules: Securing local government authorization for wall space from the Mayor
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Inclusive cross-section of local society, from authority figures to marginalized groups; U6 norms social capital: A shared sense of pride, local ownership, and community inclusion; U9 technology used: A permanent black and white mural representing the whole community
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Dignity, national recognition, and a completely peaceful civic event in an historically tense area

p1_justification: The mural represents a material, affirmative project designed to overcome community alienation and lack of belonging in a marginalized suburb by offering a highly inclusive and hopeful alternative where every segment of local society is represented.
p1_verbatim_evidence: in this neighbourhood, in the suburbs of Paris... to recognise that this place, that is forgotten, that is not heard, where the people don’t feel they belong to the French community. For me that’s one of the great ways to bring dignity back to people, by making them part of history.

p3_items.justification: The project cultivates a form of active hope and dignity in a tense, neglected neighborhood, counteracting alienation and despair by allowing residents to see themselves represented as a vital, proud part of history.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: you should have seen how people were so proud to be part of this neighbourhood... For me that’s one of the great ways to bring dignity back to people, by making them part of history.

post_title: Sarah Corbett on Craftivism and the imagination
location_string: UK and globally distributed (Shetland, Ghent, Skype)

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Crafts and Activism (Handicrafts); RS9 location: UK and international
ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Craftivist Collective; GS3 network structure: Distributed network of individuals using kits, locally organized workshops, and online connections; GS5 operational rules: Guided critical thinking through 'Crafterthought' questions integrated into repetitive hand crafts
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: Varies from solo crafters to groups of 70; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Often shy, introverted, anxious, or burnt-out activists who may not see themselves as traditionally creative; U4 location: Distributed globally; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Sarah Corbett; U6 norms social capital: Gentle protest focusing on positive visions, compassion, and non-confrontational listening; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: Using hand memory, meditative repetition, and structured guides to foster self-reflection rather than rigid dogma; U9 technology used: Handicrafts, stitching, paper craft, and written materials (kits)
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Facilitating conversation side-by-side with lowered eye contact to reduce pressure and conflict; I3 deliberation processes: Slowing down and reflecting in a calm environment where introverts feel safe and talkative members share space; I4 conflicts among users: Non-confrontational discussion of disagreements through side-by-side physical activity; I6 lobbying activities: Creating handmade gifts for powerholders to prompt pragmatic self-reflection
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Improved self-confidence, empowerment, reduced anxiety, depression, and overwhelm

p1_justification: The practice focuses on physically manifesting a positive vision of the world through craft, framing it as an affirmative, compassionate alternative that empowers participants and helps overcome alienation, anxiety, and burnout.
p1_verbatim_evidence: my approach to Craftivism is ‘gentle protest’. You’re protesting against something but you always focus on what you want in the world, a positive vision of the world, and you’re using craft as a tool to do gentle protest which is careful, considerate and compassionate activism

p3_items.justification: The practice actively cultivates hope and a belief in the possibility of a better future by requiring participants to focus on positive visions of the world rather than just focusing on what they are protesting against.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: You’re protesting against something but you always focus on what you want in the world, a positive vision of the world

p3_items.justification: It integrates physical activity with critical thinking prompts, encouraging participants to develop critical consciousness and self-reflection while working, which leads to active and mindful personal empowerment.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: once you’re doing the repetitive hand action... then I have little ‘Crafterthought’ questions for people to think through. Like critical thinking, so using the time of working with your hands to think.

p1_justification: The practice presents Venice's urban design as a concrete, material alternative to modern car-dominated spaces, aiming to transform built environments to overcome cognitive alienation and support human flourishing.
p1_verbatim_evidence: Our built environments have a key role to play in such a transformation... Venice is a place that has evolved in such a way that absolutely stokes the curiosity. Every step fires it up... puts us into an altered state we’re not used to, one in which our attention can flourish.

p3_items.justification: The practice focuses on leveraging the physical layout of the city to stimulate human curiosity and provoke a shift in attention and cognitive state, acting as a form of sensory and imaginative provocation.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: Venice is a place that has evolved in such a way that absolutely stokes the curiosity. Every step fires it up... puts us into an altered state we’re not used to, one in which our attention can flourish.

post_title: This beer kills pipelines

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Rivers and freshwater resources; RS2 clarity of system boundaries: An extensive network of over 860 rivers including the 2km wide St Lawrence River in the province of Quebec.; RS4 human constructed facilities: Local microbreweries and the proposed 6,400km TransCanada Energy East oil pipeline.; RS9 location: Quebec, Canada along the proposed route of the Energy East pipeline.
ostrom_summary.governance: GS1 government organizations: The Canadian federal government headed by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.; GS2 nongovernment organizations: Coule Pas Chez Nous (a campaigning organization, later registered as a Foundation).; GS3 network structure: A collaborative coalition consisting of citizens, craft breweries, students, unions, and First Nations peoples.; GS4 property rights systems: Indigenous territorial rights (First Nations peoples' land).; GS5 operational rules: Canadian law mandating translation of corporate development applications into French.
ostrom_summary.users: U1 number of users: Over 25,000 demonstration participants, 12,000+ financial donors, and dozens of collaborating brewers.; U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: French-speaking, independent-minded citizens of Quebec including students, union members, First Nations peoples, and craft brewers.; U4 location: Quebec, Canada; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Marie-Eve Leclerc and a student leader who donated a major prize.; U6 norms social capital: High level of solidarity, collaborative cross-sector organizing, and a shared community focus on protecting local rivers.; U7 knowledge of SES mental models: A shared understanding that local water security is directly threatened by the oil pipeline and is foundational to community life, local industries (beer), and general well-being.; U8 importance of resource: Extremely high, prioritized directly over industrial energy pipeline projects.
ostrom_summary.interactions: I2 information sharing among users: Utilizing specialized craft beer packaging (bottles, casks, mats), posters, t-shirts, and oil-covered duck stickers placed on currency.; I3 deliberation processes: Collaborative project design and collective recipe creation.; I4 conflicts among users: Conflict between Quebec's citizen/campaign movement and TransCanada corporation over the risks of the Energy East pipeline.; I5 investment activities: Crowdfunding $400,000 from public donations, and generating over $15,000 through the sale of the campaign beer where $1 of every purchase is donated back to the campaign.; I6 lobbying activities: Direct distribution of campaign materials/beer to political parties and confronting the CEO of TransCanada.; I7 self organizing activities: Grassroots self-organization of the 'Coule Pas Chez Nous' coalition, mobilizing massive demonstrations, and launching a multi-brewery collaborative protest beer.; I8 networking activities: Enrolling 26 craft breweries along the pipeline route and using the project to connect with populations outside traditional environmental movements.
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Successfully keeping the pipeline on hold, raising over $15,000, selling out multiple batches of beer, and generating widespread national awareness.

p1_justification: The campaign champions an affirmative, community-based vision grounded in the material preservation of local rivers and collective joy, presenting a hopeful and holistic alternative to corporate resource exploitation.
p1_verbatim_evidence: No water, no beer. No beer, no fun... It’s not a project that talks about climate change... It’s about water. And fun. It’s about having a beer for a cause, and it reaches into wider networks.

p3_items.justification: The project cultivates active community agency and hope by reframing environmental resistance around positive solidarity and fun, thereby preventing defeatist attitudes in the face of major industrial development.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: It’s about water. And fun. It’s about having a beer for a cause, and it reaches into wider networks. One old man I met (and he was not sensitive to environmental issues) said 'they can’t touch my beer!'

p3_items.justification: The campaign uses powerful, cautionary symbolic imagery to warn the public of the disastrous ecological consequences that a pipeline failure would bring to local environments.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: They printed 800,000 stickers showing a duck covered in oil, and distributed them inviting people to put them onto the $1 bank bills...

post_title: Podcast: An afternoon playing in the street

ostrom_summary.resource_system: RS1 sector: Urban public square / street play; RS4 human constructed facilities: A public town square; RS9 location: the Rotherfold, a square at the top of the town
ostrom_summary.governance: GS2 nongovernment organizations: Occupy Play and Network of Wellbeing; GS5 operational rules: Flexible, negotiable rules for street games; GS6 collective choice rules: Collaborative, self-negotiated rules among the children
ostrom_summary.users: U2 socioeconomic attributes of users: Children, parents, and refugees from Africa and Pakistan; U5 leadership entrepreneurship: Inez Aponte, Toni Spencer, and Ian Blackwell; U6 norms social capital: Compromise, sharing, collaboration, and resolving disagreements; U9 technology used: Simple toys without batteries or wifi (e.g. skipping ropes, balls, chalk)
ostrom_summary.interactions: I3 deliberation processes: Negotiation and adaptation of street game rules; I7 self organizing activities: A group of locals organizing the street games festival; I8 networking activities: Collaboration and support from local organizations
ostrom_summary.outcomes: O1 social performance measures: Lively communal activity, laughter, kids learning rules, and adults participating

p1_justification: The festival acts as an affirmative, real-world alternative to the alienation of modern technology-driven lifestyles, offering a collaborative, low-tech reclamation of public space that fosters community and inclusion.
p1_verbatim_evidence: Last Sunday, a group of people in Totnes organised the first Totnes Street Games Festival, an afternoon of games and play in the Rotherfold, a square at the top of the town. ... You don’t need to buy much in order to play in the street. A skipping rope perhaps. Or a ball. A cricket bat maybe. A piece of chalk. Certainly nothing that needed any batteries. Nothing that needed a wifi connection.

p3_items.justification: By engaging in street games with adaptable rules, children are transformed from passive consumers of pre-programmed digital stories into active subjects who collaborate and co-create their own play experience.
p3_items.verbatim_evidence: It was fascinating to see how naturally most of the kids in the Rotherfold slipped into the mode of playing together, of learning the rules and then adapting them. ... Kids learn to compromise, to share, to collaborate, to resolve disagreements. The rules are negotiable, rather than most computer games where you are playing someone else’s story, with rules that are fixed.
